header.title: SentinelAPI
header.subtitle: Intelligent API gateway with ML-powered anomaly detection and automated threat response

summary.plain_english: An API gateway layer that sits in front of any backend service and learns normal traffic patterns using machine learning. When requests deviate from baseline — unusual volumes, geographic anomalies, credential-stuffing patterns — the system automatically rate-limits, blocks, or escalates the threat without human intervention.

motivation.quote: Most API security tooling is rule-based and brittle — you write a rule, attackers adapt, you update the rule. I wanted to build something that detects novel attack patterns by learning what normal looks like, then flagging deviations. That requires marrying real-time stream processing with ML inference at the edge.

architecture.flow_summary: Client → CloudFront → API Gateway → Lambda authorizer → Kinesis stream → ML inference (SageMaker) → WAF rule updates → DynamoDB threat log

items: Every incoming API request is scored for anomalies before it reaches the origin — a Lambda authorizer loads a lightweight model from a Lambda layer and runs inline inference, adding under 15ms to the request path while blocking high-confidence threats at the gateway

Request traffic is streamed continuously for asynchronous ML analysis — metadata for every request (IP, endpoint, latency, status code) is published to Kinesis Data Streams at ingest time, decoupling collection from inference so high-traffic bursts never stall the request path

Novel attack patterns are detected without predefined rules — a SageMaker-hosted Isolation Forest model trained on historical traffic baselines identifies statistical outliers in the Kinesis stream; new attack signatures are flagged even if they've never been seen before

Confirmed threat IPs are automatically blocked at the CloudFront edge — a Lambda consumer writes high-confidence anomalies to a WAF IP set on a fixed batching schedule, cutting off malicious traffic before it reaches API Gateway with no manual intervention

Alerts are severity-tiered to keep operator noise low — low-confidence anomalies are written to DynamoDB for scheduled review; high-confidence threats immediately trigger SNS alerts to an ops topic so on-call engineers are only paged for events that require action

Cold start latency for the Lambda authorizer — loading an ML model from S3 on every cold start added 800ms+ of latency. Solved by packaging the model into a Lambda layer and keeping the function warm with provisioned concurrency for the critical path

Feature engineering at stream time — converting raw request logs into model-ready features (rolling averages, geo-distance deltas, endpoint entropy) required stateful aggregation inside the Kinesis consumer, which complicated exactly-once processing guarantees

WAF rule update rate limits — AWS WAF imposes update frequency limits on IP sets. Batching threat IPs and applying updates on a fixed schedule (rather than per-event) was necessary to avoid throttling during attack bursts
